title: The Hospies

jokes.text: Moira's opening monologue: 'I think surprise parties are tacky, and far too often organized by well-intentioned people with very bad taste. But... Patrick has always wanted one, poor thing. So I am throwing him a surprise party... tonight.'
jokes.setup: Moira establishes herself as someone who finds surprise parties beneath her taste standards
jokes.punchline: She's throwing one anyway — 'poor thing' perfectly encapsulates her condescending affection
jokes.explanation: The irony of Moira decrying bad taste while organizing the very thing she mocks is perfectly in character. 'Poor thing' as applied to a grown man wanting a birthday party is peak Moira condescension — affectionate and withering simultaneously. High character integration because this attitude belongs exclusively to Moira.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would never throw a surprise party. They're tacky, they're intrusive, and quite frankly, they reek of desperation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's a poor thing to say.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm organizing one right now.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'When I planned that Casablanca-themed party for your mother's 40th, I had to quarantine the camels for a month.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is cautioning David about the logistics of throwing a last-minute party
jokes.punchline: The 'quarantine the camels' detail — a perfectly casual aside that reveals the insane scale of Rose family parties in their wealthy past
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy and economy. The camel quarantine is delivered completely straight, as if it's the most reasonable thing in the world. It's specific (not just 'hired elephants'), it uses real-world logic (quarantine), and it perfectly encapsulates the Rose family's absurd former wealth. The specificity of 'a month' sells the commitment to the bit. This approaches the 8.0 anchor — sharp, unexpected, and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, when you're planning a party at this level, you have to think about every detail. I've already arranged for the camel quarantine facility.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Camels? Johnny, we're in Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, and they'll be quarantined. Properly. I won't have them arriving infected.

jokes.text: David: 'I've actually been organizing this for quite some time, I'm just telling you right now because I know that none of you can keep a secret.'
jokes.setup: The family challenges David on the last-minute nature of the party
jokes.punchline: David deflects by insulting the family's ability to keep secrets — simultaneously defending himself and attacking them
jokes.explanation: Solid David Rose line — the self-serving logic that he's keeping them out of the loop for their own good is very in character. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't trust you people with secrets. I once told my mother I was thinking about getting bangs, and by the time I got home, the entire country club knew about it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh! At 9 a.m. it's a little early for character assassination.'
jokes.setup: David has implied the family cannot keep secrets
jokes.punchline: Moira treats being called untrustworthy as a time-of-day offense rather than a moral one
jokes.explanation: Very good line. The implication is she'd be fine with character assassination at a more civilized hour. Pure Moira: dramatic, self-important, and oddly specific. Economy is excellent — it lands hard in very few words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Character assassination? David, I've been called worse things by my own reflection.

jokes.text: The Crows movie secret: David immediately told everyone how the movie ended despite being sworn to secrecy — 'You told me the day you got home. You gave me the script.'
jokes.setup: David is arguing that his family can't keep secrets
jokes.punchline: The family immediately produces evidence that David himself cannot keep secrets, specifically about Moira's movie
jokes.explanation: Neat reversal — David accuses the family and is immediately indicted. The 'you gave me the script' escalation is a good comedic beat. Solid structural payoff.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I knew you all went to see it without me.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, we were going to tell you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your mother may have mentioned the ending.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I didn't mention it, I described it. In detail. For forty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: See? Secrets. This is exactly what I'm talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but to be fair, we didn't know you hadn't seen it yet.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And your mother may have spoiled it for the entire theater.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They were very engaged.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And you were both sworn to secrecy, so David is right not to trust you.' — Then immediately reveals the extra surprise.
jokes.setup: Moira defends David's secretiveness and validates his distrust of the family
jokes.punchline: In the same breath, she reveals 'there is an extra surprise tonight' — proving she is ALSO incapable of keeping a secret
jokes.explanation: The structure here is tight: Moira sides with David, proving his point about others, and then immediately undercuts her own argument. The self-incrimination is perfectly timed and entirely in character — Moira is constitutionally incapable of not sharing exciting news.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not ready for this. I'm not emotionally prepared. What if they don't like me? What if I say something weird? What if they ask me questions about myself and I have to talk about my feelings?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Yes, things are certainly racing along at a dangerously rapid velocity.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says meeting the parents is 'a big step'
jokes.punchline: Johnny's overwrought phrasing — 'dangerously rapid velocity' — is technically redundant and pompous but delivered straight, revealing his nervousness
jokes.explanation: Johnny's barely-contained anxiety expressed in absurdly formal language is a reliable comedic register for him. The redundancy of 'dangerously rapid velocity' is the joke — he's trying to sound measured while clearly alarmed. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not going to spoil it for you, but let's just say the ending is a real 'crowvergence.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A crowvergence. Crow plus convergence.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not a word.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It is now. The climax is an absolute 'crowpocalypse.'

jokes.text: 'Crowpocalypse. But you didn't hear it from me.' — Alexis reveals the Crows movie ending she was sworn to keep secret.
jokes.setup: Johnny asked how the Crows movie ended; the family was sworn to secrecy
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately tells him 'Crowpocalypse' and adds 'but you didn't hear it from me' — the caveat is meaningless since she just told him
jokes.explanation: This is the perfect payoff to the running joke that nobody can keep a secret. The word 'Crowpocalypse' is itself funny (Moira's movie titles are always gloriously overwrought), and 'but you didn't hear it from me' is a classic empty disclaimer. Alexis's complete lack of self-awareness sells it. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is a real special moment, Johnny. I'm honored to be part of it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're not part of it, Roland. You're just avoiding going back to work.

jokes.text: Roland: 'This is a really big deal, Johnny. I mean, meeting my best friend's son's boyfriend's parents for the very first time, I am very touched that you wanted me to share this moment with you.'
jokes.setup: Roland appears at the motel, clearly uninvited
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals Roland is there because Johnny asked him to mow the lawn three times and he never did — so his 'presence' is accidental
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious insertion into a meaningful moment is quintessential Roland. The six-degrees-of-separation relationship description ('my best friend's son's boyfriend's parents') is a nice absurdist escalation. The punchline that he's there by accident rather than invitation is a solid subversion.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, look at him. He's handsome, he's successful, he's got good taste. I'm just a simple business man who sells things out of a warehouse. So yeah, I could see how you'd think that.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I asked you to mow the lawn three times, Roland, so you being here is more your decision than mine.'
jokes.setup: Roland is touched to be 'included' in this intimate moment
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies Roland is here purely because he failed to do his job, not by invitation
jokes.explanation: Johnny's delivery of this is the key — it's exasperated but not cruel, and the logic of 'your being here is your decision, not mine' is a funny reframe of Roland's passive incompetence. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, you've got your mother's eyes, and your father's... everything else.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Oh, no, no! I do get that all the time, though.' — Roland is flattered to be mistaken for David's father.
jokes.setup: Clint has mistaken Roland for David's sophisticated father
jokes.punchline: Roland, with complete sincerity, says he gets mistaken for David's father all the time — which would be mortifying to David
jokes.explanation: The implication that people regularly mistake this man for David Rose's father — and Roland's genuine pleasure at this — is a layered joke. It's funny because: (1) Roland doesn't understand the insult, (2) it implies Johnny looks bad, (3) it's absurd that this happens 'all the time.' Economy is excellent — one short sentence does enormous comic work.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, he doesn't *run* it, per se. I mean, the place basically runs itself. Johnny just sort of... exists there.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'It's uh, you know, you've got David's handsome looks there, and then my face.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is correcting the mistaken identity and introducing himself as David's actual father
jokes.punchline: He self-deprecatingly implies his own face is the less attractive option in the equation
jokes.explanation: Competent self-deprecating humor from Johnny. The rhythm is good but the joke itself is fairly generic — any father could say this. Not particularly specific to Johnny Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look, Roland, I know you didn't mean anything by it. We all say things we don't mean sometimes. The point is, we're here, we're together, and that's what matters. Sure, most days are a complete nightmare, but every now and then... it's only moderately terrible.

jokes.text: Roland: 'More like, runs it into the ground.' — responding to Clint's comment that Johnny 'runs' the motel.
jokes.setup: Clint compliments Johnny by noting he runs the motel
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately undercuts Johnny in front of his son's boyfriend's parents — seemingly without malice
jokes.explanation: The economy is great — five words do real damage. The joke is Roland's obliviousness: he probably thinks he's being charming or funny rather than sabotaging Johnny's first impression. Somewhat predictable Roland behavior but executed well.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You two need to talk about your relationship.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Our relationship? What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I'm just saying, people are starting to talk.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: People are talking about us? Why would they—

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The business partnership. It's a mess. You two can't keep bickering like this or the store's going under.

jokes.text: Oh, we do have a lot of fun around here, don't we? / We do, sometimes.
jokes.setup: Roland tries to walk back his insult by spinning it as workplace banter
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat 'We do, sometimes' is a masterclass in damning with faint praise — not quite agreeing, not denying, just barely managing a smile
jokes.explanation: Economy is the star here. Three words that contain volumes of resigned Johnny Rose energy. The pause before 'sometimes' does the work.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I'm just going to say what everyone's thinking. You two look like you've seen a ghost.

jokes.text: Roland implies Johnny is 'not as progressive as the rest of us' about David and Patrick's relationship — Johnny corrects him that he was talking about mixing business and romance.
jokes.setup: Johnny awkwardly mentions being 'a skeptic about those two'; Roland helpfully explains it's a 'generational thing'
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies he meant the business relationship — but the damage to first impressions may already be done
jokes.explanation: This is a nice cringe-comedy beat. Roland's oblivious 'clarification' that Johnny has a generational problem with gay relationships — said to Patrick's parents — is exquisitely awkward. The correction doesn't fully undo the discomfort, which is exactly right.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, this is awkward.

jokes.text: Clint and Marci's stunned reaction when they realize Johnny may have just revealed Patrick is in a relationship with David — the 'I missed the last part of what you just said' pause.
jokes.setup: Clint asks if Johnny knew they were business partners — establishing they don't know Patrick is gay
jokes.punchline: Johnny's stammering and the Brewers' stunned silence signals the information has just landed catastrophically
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is pure cringe — watching Johnny realize he's accidentally outed Patrick to his own parents. 'I missed the last part of what you just said' is a hilariously human attempt to pretend you didn't understand what you absolutely understood. Strong dramatic irony for the audience who knows what's happening.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about potential revenue streams, and I believe we should consider monetizing our bodies through clinical trials and medical testing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We could be compensated handsomely for our participation in pharmaceutical studies, cosmetic research, or experimental treatments. It's really quite the entrepreneurial opportunity.

jokes.text: Johnny's deadpan talking-head observation: 'It kinda seems like they didn't know their son is in a relationship with David.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just accidentally outed Patrick to his parents at the motel check-in
jokes.punchline: In his confessional, Johnny understates the catastrophic situation with studied calm
jokes.explanation: Johnny's talent for gentle understatement shines here. The gap between what happened (accidentally outed his son's boyfriend to that boyfriend's parents on their first meeting) and how he describes it ('kinda seems like they didn't know') is the joke. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't attend fan conventions because I find the environment deeply unsettling. The fluorescent lighting, the industrial carpeting, the sheer density of human bodies pressed against one another like salamander people emerging from a primordial swamp. They're damp, they're pallid, they smell of corn chips and desperation, and they move in that peculiar, sideways fashion—always angling for a photograph, a signature, some token of acknowledgment that I exist in their reality. It's nightmarish.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You're looking especially fresh and photogenic today, Mother.' — setting up a pitch
jokes.setup: Alexis is about to pitch Moira on something she won't want to do
jokes.punchline: The transparent flattery immediately signals to both Moira and the audience that manipulation is incoming — Moira's 'Why thank you, Alexis. Perhaps it's my excellent news' response shows she's playing the game too
jokes.explanation: Functional character comedy. The Alexis-Moira dynamic of mutual manipulation is well established, and this is a clean setup for the scene. Not especially original — it's a standard 'transparent flattery before a pitch' beat.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were a perfume model, Mom. You literally just stood there smelling things.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Then you were a weather girl, then you did that thing on that cruise ship...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And then you were in a Mutt Schitt commercial where you licked a toilet.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So I think you've hit rock bottom. And then you kept digging.

jokes.text: Moira's medical trial plan: 'According to the radio, there's a darling little medical clinic in Elm Grove. Where if any one of us, say, you, or your father, participates in a few nugatory tests, we could be bringing in over $200 a week!'
jokes.setup: Moira says she's found a 'promising revenue stream' to address their financial deficit from Cabaret
jokes.punchline: She's proposing that her husband and daughter participate in medical trials — 'a few nugatory tests' — and she's cheerfully volunteering others, not herself
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — the word 'nugatory' (meaning trivial/worthless) to describe medical trials, the cheerful specificity of volunteering everyone but herself, and the deflation of '$200 a week' as a revenue stream for someone who once quarantined camels. Character integration is high.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I suppose I can make an appearance. Though I must say, the asymmetrical faces in these convention photographs are rather... distressing.

jokes.text: Alexis cuts off Moira's medical trial pitch with: 'Or, you could make $2,000 in one afternoon.' — pivot to the fan convention
jokes.setup: Moira has been pitching $200/week from medical trials
jokes.punchline: Alexis counters with $2,000 in one afternoon — immediately making Moira's scheme look absurd
jokes.explanation: Clean economic pivot. The contrast of $200/week versus $2,000 in an afternoon is the joke, and the pause before the reveal is correctly deployed. Good setup economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'll tell Patrick I have a very contagious skin condition and he can't come near me for at least two weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, you literally just had him over for dinner last night.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I haven't hit rock bottom quite yet. It's a matter of self-worth.'
jokes.setup: Moira is explaining why she won't do the convention
jokes.punchline: She frames not doing the convention as a dignified stance — moments before capitulating
jokes.explanation: The irony of 'I haven't hit rock bottom quite yet' — implying she has previously been near rock bottom but is managing — is good Moira. The self-worth framing while accepting a convention gig for money is classic dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I wasn't at the gym.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're wearing a tank top and shorts.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was gardening.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're drenched in sweat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's hot out.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You did a commercial for adult diapers! In Japan! In full-face Kabuki makeup!'
jokes.setup: Moira has said she hasn't 'hit rock bottom' yet
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately produces evidence of past bottom-hitting — the triple escalation of 'adult diapers / in Japan / in Kabuki makeup' each adds a layer of indignity
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating structure. The three-beat construction — each clause more specific and undignified than the last — is textbook comedy writing. 'In Japan' is funny alone (the implication being it was only acceptable if hidden geographically), but adding 'in full-face Kabuki makeup' is inspired because it raises a question: was the makeup to hide her identity or was it part of the brief? The ambiguity is rich. Justifies 8+ — this is comparable in craft to the anchor examples.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you see, Stevie and I have a very... special arrangement. We're not exactly dating, but we're also not exactly not dating. It's more of a... situational companionship with occasional physical intimacy.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, stop talking.

jokes.text: Moira's 'safe word' plan if the fans get overwhelming: 'There will be a safe word in case the gaggle of asymmetrical faces gets to be too much.'
jokes.setup: Moira has been persuaded to attend the convention
jokes.punchline: 'A gaggle of asymmetrical faces' — another brilliantly specific cruel Moira-ism for fans, and the concept of needing a safe word for a fan convention
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'salamander people' works as a running character pattern — Moira's fan descriptions get progressively more surreal. 'Asymmetrical faces' is a different flavor of cruelty than 'salamander people' (it's clinical rather than zoological), and needing a safe word for a fan event is an absurd status-protection measure only Moira would require.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No, absolutely not!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, that's exactly what we think.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, pretty much.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, so I told Patrick that there's a salmonella outbreak at the café, so he will not be coming here until I tell him to tonight.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining how he's keeping Patrick away for the surprise party
jokes.punchline: The cover story is 'salmonella outbreak at the café' — an alarming and reputationally devastating lie
jokes.explanation: David's tendency to choose lies that create enormous collateral damage is funny and character-specific. The immediate follow-up from the café owner makes it land better.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Everyone, just calm down and don't say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think what David is trying to say is...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, Dad, that's the opposite of what I just said.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Is it possible you could maybe... choose a lie that doesn't make the café look like it's in violation of health codes?'
jokes.setup: David has announced his salmonella cover story
jokes.punchline: Twyla's mild, reasonable objection — framed as a small request rather than an outrage — is the joke; she's accepting the lie as a given and just asking for a better one
jokes.explanation: Twyla's placid acceptance of the situation while making a reasonable business request is very her. The comedy is in what she DOESN'T object to — she has no problem with the lie itself, only its professional implications for her café.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've protected you from all the dark underbelly of this industry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, I've been sexually harassed at every job I've ever had.

jokes.text: David: 'I think it's a pretty good one.' — defending the salmonella lie
jokes.setup: Twyla has asked for a less damaging lie
jokes.punchline: David defends the salmonella cover story with complete sincerity, as if he's proud of it
jokes.explanation: Compact and effective. David's genuine pride in a deeply irresponsible lie is very in character. Short but punchy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Gloria Gregson is a holistic practitioner who believes in the healing power of human touch. She's developed a method called 'Sensual Awakening Through Intentional Contact.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That sounds like a euphemism.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's not a euphemism, David. It's a legitimate therapeutic modality. Gloria has helped countless clients achieve spiritual and physical harmony through controlled, consensual touch sessions.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So she just... touches people?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Precisely. It's very scientific. Gloria has charts and everything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm concerned about the liability on this one, Mom.

jokes.text: David: 'What? They're not supposed to be here for another 11 minutes!'
jokes.setup: Johnny tells David that Patrick's parents have already checked in
jokes.punchline: 'Another 11 minutes' — the specificity of David's scheduling panic is characteristic; he's treated this like a military operation
jokes.explanation: The '11 minutes' specificity is a quick character note — David's controlling anxiety expressed as precise scheduling. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: You know, you really do look like Moira's sister.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: *looks disgusted*

jokes.text: Johnny's 'romantically in business with each other' phrasing — and David's disgusted 'Ew!'
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to describe David and Patrick's relationship without saying 'romantic'
jokes.punchline: He accidentally coins 'romantically in business with each other' — a hilariously awkward corporate euphemism for being in a relationship — and David's 'Ew!' is a reaction to the phrasing itself
jokes.explanation: Johnny's corporate vocabulary bleeding into personal life is a reliable and well-deployed character note. 'Romantically in business with each other' is an absurd phrase that makes perfect sense coming from Johnny — and David's 'Ew' reacting to the PHRASING rather than the revelation is a nice misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I brought flowers and pizza. The pizza is for eating.

jokes.text: David: 'They call the store all the time! I mean, what do they think, I'm just his business partner?!' — Johnny and Alexis: 'Possibly.' / 'I mean, yeah.'
jokes.setup: David is incredulous that Patrick's parents don't know about them
jokes.punchline: Johnny and Alexis's simultaneous 'possibly'/'yeah' confirming that yes, they're probably being perceived as just business partners
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the gentleness with which Johnny and Alexis confirm David's worst fear. 'Possibly' and 'I mean, yeah' are both hedged confirmations that are simultaneously kind and devastating. The parallel delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you talk to your parents?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No, they're... they're in Toronto.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, they're at the motel.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A motel in Toronto.

jokes.text: Possibly. / I mean, yeah.
jokes.setup: David asks rhetorically 'What do they think, I'm just his business partner?!'
jokes.punchline: Both Johnny and Roland confirm, simultaneously, that yes, that is exactly what they think — their overlapping agreement is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Two words (three including 'yeah') that detonate the scene. The synchronized confirmation from both the emotionally intelligent (Johnny) and the oblivious (Roland) character — different registers, same answer — makes this a very satisfying beat. Exceptional economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do your parents know about us?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: David: 'Everybody calm down! Nobody say a word to anybody!' — Alexis: 'Well, that sounds reasonable.' — David: 'What did I just say?!'
jokes.setup: David attempts to take control of the crisis
jokes.punchline: Alexis's reasonable agreement is immediately treated as a violation of the 'say nothing' edict
jokes.explanation: A tight comedy beat. David's 'What did I just say?!' applied to someone who said something completely benign (and supportive) is perfect — his control freakery has become self-defeating. The logic is immediate and satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Convention prep: 'Now, if they ask for pictures of your feet...' Alexis: 'Hailey, you have been more than helpful.'
jokes.setup: Hailey the convention organizer is briefing Moira on fan protocols
jokes.punchline: The foot photo request — casually mentioned as a normal possibility — is cut off before completion, which is funnier than hearing it finished
jokes.explanation: The interruption IS the punchline — what follows 'if they ask for pictures of your feet' would complete a sentence nobody wants completed, and cutting it off respects the audience's imagination. The setup is perfectly economic. Later in the episode this gets a callback with actual foot photo pricing, making this moment richer on rewatch.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I think it's important to come out on your own terms, you know? When you're ready. Not when someone else decides for you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, I brought home a couple in college and my mother just... she knew.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Alexis dear, now is as bad a time as any to tell you. There is a dark side to Mummy's industry, and I've tried to protect you from it your whole life.'
jokes.setup: Moira is preparing to reveal the terrors of the convention world to Alexis
jokes.punchline: The absurdity is that she's treating a fan convention as a trauma worthy of parental protection — and Alexis is a grown adult who has seen far worse
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical framing of mundane events as profound traumas is her core comedic register. 'Now is as bad a time as any' is also a funny corruption of 'as good a time as any.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have something to tell you. Patrick's throwing you a surprise party.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Which is ironic, given that Patrick is literally in the closet.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You took me to the Playboy Mansion when I was seven.' Moira: 'And you had a wonderful time in the children's grotto.'
jokes.setup: Moira claims she has 'tried to protect' Alexis from the dark side of her industry
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately cites the Playboy Mansion visit at age seven — and Moira defends it by noting there was a 'children's grotto'
jokes.explanation: Outstanding. The 'children's grotto' is the key detail — it both implies the Playboy Mansion had to accommodate children (absurd) and that Moira considers this a reasonable child-proofing measure. The specificity of Alexis being 'seven' grounds the absurdity. Moira's defense is delivered with absolute sincerity, which makes it far darker and funnier. This is among the episode's best jokes — original, character-specific, escalating, and quotable. Justifies 8+ clearly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I made it up. There was no salmonella outbreak. I just needed an excuse to not go to the opening.

jokes.text: Moira sees Gloria Gregson at the convention and panics: 'She only played Grace on Touched With Grace for 37 years. The woman has 17 Emmys for playing a hot-headed nurse who healed people with her sexual touch.'
jokes.setup: Moira desperately wants to leave before being seen at the convention
jokes.punchline: The show description — 'a hot-headed nurse who healed people with her sexual touch' — is an inspired parody of soap opera premises
jokes.explanation: Genuinely funny soap opera parody. 'Healed people with her sexual touch' is the punchline — it's an absurd show premise that perfectly skewers the genre, and 17 Emmys for it implies the industry validated it wholeheartedly. The specificity of 37 years and 17 Emmys adds layers of absurdity. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Is the party at the café?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. I told you that to get you out of the house because I needed to plan your surprise birthday party.

jokes.text: Gloria: 'I'm usually the only marquee name at these things.' Moira: 'I'm shocked by my presence as well.'
jokes.setup: Gloria is expressing delight at having a fellow 'marquee name' at the convention
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I'm shocked by my presence as well' — she's genuinely surprised and slightly horrified to be here, but plays it as a wry shared joke
jokes.explanation: This is exquisite economy. In five words, Moira conveys embarrassment, self-awareness, and aristocratic humor simultaneously. 'Shocked by my presence as well' is a line that works on two levels — she genuinely cannot believe she's there, and she's gamely performing the shared status game with Gloria. Perfect character voice. High quotability and economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I'm not in a relationship with your son. I'm married to him.

jokes.text: Alexis introduces herself: 'I am Alexis Rose, Moira's publicist and daughter.' Gloria: 'Oh! Daughter? More like sister.' Alexis: 'Ew.'
jokes.setup: Alexis introduces herself as Moira's daughter (and publicist)
jokes.punchline: Gloria's flattery ('more like sister') is immediately deflated by Alexis's disgusted 'Ew' — she finds the comparison to her mother more offensive than flattering
jokes.explanation: Three-beat comedy at its best. The flattery, the expected reaction (gratitude/embarrassment), and then the complete subversion ('Ew') — Alexis's 'Ew' is funny because it's both a rejection of being compared to her mother AND an indictment of the compliment itself. Perfect economy, very character-specific to Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, they're not... they're not *homophobic* or anything. They're just... selective about who they associate with based on a complex matrix of social standing, financial viability, and whether or not you summer in the Hamptons.

jokes.text: The extended uncomfortable silence when David asks 'Like they know about us, right?' and Patrick clearly hasn't told them — the pause from 10:22 to 10:28.
jokes.setup: David is pressing Patrick on whether his parents know about their relationship
jokes.punchline: The six-second silence where Patrick cannot say yes — punctuated by David's dawning horror
jokes.explanation: The silence is the punchline, which requires tremendous structural confidence from the writers. The audience has been watching David discover this in stages, and the pause at 10:22-10:28 is when both David and the audience fully arrive at the same understanding simultaneously. Maximum economy (zero words), enormous impact. This is exceptional TV comedy structure. Rewatch bonus is high — knowing what comes makes the silence more affecting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We just shouldn't mention that we know about the relationship.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm aware of how that sounds.

jokes.text: David: 'That's why I brought this couple home one day in college, and just told my parents to deal with it. I'm a take-charge guy, David. I like to take charge of things in my life, you know that.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to comfort Patrick about coming out to his parents by sharing his own experience
jokes.punchline: David's method of coming out — bringing home 'a couple' and demanding his parents 'deal with it' — is characteristically aggressive and unusual, and totally in character
jokes.explanation: This is a beautiful piece of character comedy that does double duty: it establishes David's pan-sexuality (a couple, not just one person) in the most David way possible, and it provides a moment of inadvertent humor amid an emotional scene. 'Deal with it' as a coming-out strategy is perfect David Rose energy. The 'I'm a take-charge guy' follow-up is self-aware irony given that he's very much NOT in charge of this situation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, wait—is that your wine? I've been using bottles of that for cheap sangria at home.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'You're gonna laugh yourself right out of the closet on this one.' — before revealing the party surprise to David
jokes.setup: Patrick has caught David upset and is about to reveal his own secret about coming out
jokes.punchline: The 'laugh yourself out of the closet' phrase works as both a literal prediction (the news is funny enough to laugh at) and a figurative metaphor for what's about to happen
jokes.explanation: A clever line that earns its place at an emotional pivot point. The wordplay is not forced — it naturally bridges the comedic and dramatic elements of the scene, which is difficult to write. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Oh honey, I've been selling feet pictures online for three years. I have a whole pricing structure. Eight dollars for a regular photo, twelve for a close-up, and twenty-five if you want me to paint my nails a specific color.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: I also offer a subscription service. Fifty dollars a month, and you get two new photos every week, plus access to my archive.

jokes.text: David, I don't think this is how surprise parties work.
jokes.setup: David has just revealed the party to Patrick before the party
jokes.punchline: Patrick's dry observation about party etiquette while processing shocking news is the definition of deadpan — his priorities seem to be gently correcting David's party mechanics
jokes.explanation: Patrick's extreme composure in the face of escalating chaos is his defining characteristic. 'I don't think this is how surprise parties work' when your parents don't know you're gay AND they're at the hotel is masterful understatement. The gap between what he's processing and what he chooses to comment on is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so people are actually paying money for pictures of feet at conventions?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well, I have very nice feet, so... fifty dollars a photo.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? Sixty. These are grade-A feet.

jokes.text: David: 'I could be just your business partner tonight. If that will help.'
jokes.setup: Patrick is processing the situation
jokes.punchline: David offers to pretend to be Patrick's 'business partner' — the very misidentification that created this crisis — as if reverting to that status is a reasonable option
jokes.explanation: A lovely callback that works emotionally and comedically. David offering to demote himself to 'business partner' — sincerely, to protect Patrick — is both funny and touching. The comedy comes from the absurdity of the regression, the emotion comes from understanding why David is willing to do it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, 'Happy Birthday' is just too complicated. It's got five syllables, multiple words, and frankly, the emotional weight is just... a lot. 'Surprise' is cleaner. One word. Gets the job done.

jokes.text: David arrives at the Brewers' room with a gift basket: 'We ran out of the body milk, so I threw in some extra lip balms.'
jokes.setup: David arrives to apologize and smooth things over with Patrick's parents
jokes.punchline: 'Body milk' as a product name, and the casual substitution of 'extra lip balms' as equivalent compensation
jokes.explanation: David's product knowledge bleeding into an apology — treating the 'body milk' shortage as a relevant detail to share with the Brewers — is character-specific. The store's inventory is relevant to him in all situations.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to excuse myself from this reunion.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Not because of any of you, but because I just realized I left my limited edition Hermès scarf in the Uber, and I need to file a claim immediately before someone tries to resell it on Vestiaire Collective.

jokes.text: Marci Brewer: 'David, we're not upset about Patrick being gay.'
jokes.setup: David has been building toward a defense of Patrick's sexuality and bracing for homophobia
jokes.punchline: The 'not upset about Patrick being gay' revelation completely reframes the scene — all of David's preparation was unnecessary
jokes.explanation: This is structurally excellent. The entire episode has been building toward a confrontation about Patrick being gay, and the misdirection — the Brewers are actually fine — works because the show earns it. The deflation of David's prepared speech is funny and genuinely moving. The simplicity of the line ('we're not upset about Patrick being gay') lands hard because it's exactly what you hope to hear.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He'll come around. Everyone does eventually. It's just a matter of time before he realizes that I'm right and he's wrong about everything.

jokes.text: David: 'Oh my God, okay! For a minute I thought this was gonna get very dark.'
jokes.setup: The Brewers have just said they're not upset about Patrick being gay
jokes.punchline: David drops his composure immediately and lets his relief show with 'very dark' — characteristically David in its bluntness
jokes.explanation: David's relief breaking through as 'very dark' is perfectly him — the phrase is understated but implies he was genuinely frightened. The 'oh my God, okay!' preamble sets up the emotional release well.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: I don't understand what he's wearing. Is that a dress?

dialogue.speaker: Marci
dialogue.line: It's fine, honey. He'll adapt.

jokes.text: Johnny to the Brewers: 'Oh. Oh, good. Good, then no one really has to feel bad about how this information came out, then, huh?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has arrived and David tells him the Brewers are fine with Patrick being gay
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate pivot to self-absolution — if nobody's upset, nobody has to feel bad about his role in accidentally revealing it
jokes.explanation: Johnny's casual self-pardoning — delivered in the relief of a positive outcome — is very true to his character. He's a good man with a tendency to let himself off the hook. The 'huh?' at the end is particularly well-calibrated: half-genuine, half-seeking confirmation.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Patrick is planning on telling you tonight. So I think the best birthday gift we could give him at this point is to just... keep him in the closet until then. I know that came out wrong, but we all understand what I'm saying, right?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is explaining the plan to the Brewers to pretend they don't know anything
jokes.punchline: 'Keep him in the closet until then' — then immediately realizing the unintentional metaphor: 'I know that came out wrong'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Keep him in the closet' is already funny because it's a tone-deaf phrasing, and 'I know that came out wrong' adds a second unintentional pun ('came out'). Johnny is aware enough to catch it but not aware enough to avoid the second pun. It's perfectly structured — the setup is innocent, the punchline is accidental, and the self-correction compounds the joke. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'We used to use that for sangria, because it was like, so cheap, and... so good.'
jokes.setup: Gloria has revealed her prestigious vineyard wine 'Touched With Grapes'
jokes.punchline: Alexis cheerfully reveals it was known to her as cheap sangria wine — simultaneously insulting and admiring
jokes.explanation: Alexis's earnest disclosure that the prestigious wine was their budget sangria is great character comedy — she means it as a compliment while clearly not realizing the implied insult. The 'so cheap, and... so good' cadence is perfectly Alexis.

jokes.text: Moira: 'So the fiscal sweetener negates any discomfiture one might feel exploiting their extremities for a $20 bill?' Gloria: '$20?! I charge $50. And just in case, I wear open-toed sandals.'
jokes.setup: Moira is asking whether the money compensates for the indignity of foot photos
jokes.punchline: Gloria's '$20?! I charge $50' correction and then 'I wear open-toed sandals' as preparation — implying she's ready for foot photo requests at any moment
jokes.explanation: The callback to the interrupted foot photo question pays off beautifully. Gloria's operational preparation — wearing open-toed sandals as standard convention wear — suggests she's made peace with this and optimized for it. The price correction ('$20?! I charge $50') adds a status competition element. The open-toed sandals detail is the best line: it implies constant readiness that is both practical and quietly tragic.

jokes.text: Gloria: 'There is a freedom in letting go, Moira.'
jokes.setup: Gloria is delivering life wisdom about her transition from chasing prestige to conventions
jokes.punchline: The grandeur of 'there is a freedom in letting go' applied to the specific context of accepting money for foot photos at fan conventions
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the zen aphorism and its specific application is the joke. Works well as a character beat for Gloria and as a pivot toward Moira's acceptance.

jokes.text: Convention rules: 'It's 30 seconds each, tickets in hand, or it's back of the line, and no, she will not be clarifying any Sunrise Bay plot points.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is laying out the fan meet-and-greet rules
jokes.punchline: The 'she will not be clarifying any Sunrise Bay plot points' — implying fans have asked and it has been a problem
jokes.explanation: Alexis as publicist in full control is funny because she's thrown herself into the role completely. The 'plot points' rule implies hard-won experience with difficult fans. Solid.

jokes.text: 'Also, we're going with Surprise. Happy birthday was too wordy, and we couldn't nail it at rehearsal.'
jokes.setup: David is giving final instructions before Patrick arrives for the surprise party
jokes.punchline: 'Happy birthday was too wordy' — they've rehearsed this and still found 'Happy Birthday' too logistically complex
jokes.explanation: This is excellent. 'Happy birthday was too wordy' implies a level of incompetence in the party guests that is perfectly absurd — two words being too many. 'We couldn't nail it at rehearsal' confirms they actually practiced shouting 'Happy Birthday' and failed. Only David would rehearse a surprise party, and only this group would fail at two words. Strong character integration and economy. Earns its 8+.

jokes.text: Johnny's awkward 'Yep, I'm just glad you could make it, Mr. and Mrs. Brewer' while knowing the full context
jokes.setup: The Brewers have arrived at the party, knowing everything but pretending not to
jokes.punchline: Johnny's stilted delivery of a normal pleasantry while carrying enormous amounts of secret knowledge is cringe comedy through understatement
jokes.explanation: The performance carries this. Johnny knowing everything and having to pretend to know nothing creates gentle dramatic irony. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: David: 'But might I recommend the crab cakes? I over-ordered, and they cost a small fortune.'
jokes.setup: David is trying to give Patrick and his parents space for a private moment
jokes.punchline: He can't exit without making the party economics immediately someone else's problem — the crab cake plug is hilariously self-interested
jokes.explanation: Pure David Rose. Even in an emotionally significant moment he cannot help but address the crab cake situation. 'They cost a small fortune' as a sales pitch is wonderfully backwards — it's an incentive to help him not waste money, not a recommendation based on quality.

jokes.text: Clint: 'I like him. I like him a lot.' Then: 'I don't understand his clothes, but...' Marci: 'Ah, you will in time.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has just come out to his parents and they have accepted him and David
jokes.punchline: Clint's 'I don't understand his clothes' is a gentle, human landing on something real — followed by Marci's reassuring 'you will in time'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's warmest and most effective joke. Placed at the emotional climax, Clint's reservation about David's clothes is the perfect human-scale concern — he has no issue with his son being gay, he just doesn't get the wardrobe. 'You will in time' suggests ongoing relationship with David, which is itself a form of acceptance. The tone perfectly captures Schitt's Creek's brand of warm comedy. Economy is exceptional — six words carry enormous emotional weight. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'David, I know my parents. They're not good actors.'
jokes.setup: David has just denied knowing anything
jokes.punchline: Patrick's quiet, calm exposure of the deception — the 'not good actors' assessment is both specific and affectionate
jokes.explanation: Patrick's dry certainty is a great comedic register for him. 'They're not good actors' as the specific tell is funny — he knows his parents, he knows David, he sees through both. Economy is tight.

jokes.text: Patrick dismantles David's cover: 'And the gift basket that you gave them that I'm hoping you paid for, that had nothing to do with you trying to smooth things over with them?'
jokes.setup: Patrick is walking through all the evidence of David's intervention
jokes.punchline: 'I'm hoping you paid for' — a parenthetical aside establishing that David's gift-giving habit sometimes involves store merchandise he didn't technically purchase
jokes.explanation: The parenthetical 'I'm hoping you paid for' is a lovely character note — Patrick knows David well enough that this question needs to be asked, and it adds a layer of gentle accountability while still being affectionate. Good character integration.

jokes.text: David's confession: 'It was just a very messy day, and I was, I was trying to detangle things, and... and just make everything okay.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has laid out all the evidence of David's meddling
jokes.punchline: 'Detangle' as a metaphor for the chaos David created — a word from their skincare/hair world applied to a deeply human crisis
jokes.explanation: 'Detangle' in this context is a characterful word choice — David's vocabulary bleeding from his professional world into personal crisis management. Not a standout joke but a nice character texture moment.

jokes.text: Not to step on the moment, but uh, I do have to lock up in five.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick have just had a tender reconciliation moment
jokes.punchline: Twyla intrudes on the romantic scene with a completely practical café-closing concern
jokes.explanation: A perfect Twyla exit joke. Her 'not to step on the moment' acknowledgment that she IS stepping on the moment, followed by the completely banal business concern, is character comedy at its most efficient. Twyla's cheerful obliviousness to emotional registers is her defining trait, and this line captures it perfectly.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Stevie attend the regional hospitality awards, while Moira does everything in her power to stop Alexis from auditioning for Cabaret.
insight: Character deadpan carries a solid mid-tier episode through 45 jokes in forty-five minutes.
